title: Lipstick on Silk
stainChemistry: Lipstick is a four-component stain: pigment (iron oxides, lakes), oil (castor oil, lanolin), wax (beeswax, carnauba), and dye (D&C reds). On silk, the wax creates a hydrophobic shell around the pigment, the oil penetrates the fiber, and the dye transfers color. Treatment must break through the wax barrier to access the underlying components.
whyThisWorks: On silk, the treatment addresses components in order: first dissolve the wax/oil matrix with POG or solvent, then lift the pigment with surfactant, then address any remaining dye with oxidizer. Scraping excess first prevents spreading. Working from the back of the fabric (when possible) pushes the stain out rather than deeper in.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply POG to dissolve the oil/wax component
2. Apply NSD to lift dissolved residue and pigment
3. If color remains: apply sodium hydrosulfite on colorfast fabrics — 5-10 min dwell time, monitor
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse
warnings: No peroxide on silk — use sodium hydrosulfite (reducing agent) instead

steps: 1. Scrape or blot excess — do NOT rub (spreading worsens it)
2. Apply Dawn dish soap directly to the stain. Work gently
3. Blot with clean white cloth. Repeat Dawn application until stain fades
4. Rinse with warm water (unless protein component present — then cold)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 rounds of treatment. Combo stains are inherently harder — a professional cleaner has specialized chemistry for each component
warnings: Always test on a hidden seam or inside hem first — silk dyes may not tolerate spotting agents.
Treat each component in order — don't skip steps
Cold water always — heat sets protein components permanently

neverDo: No protease enzymes — silk IS protein; enzymes digest the fiber
No chlorine bleach — dissolves protein fibers
No alkaline detergents (pH >8) — breaks peptide bonds
No acetone — bleaches and damages fiber structure
fiberSensitivities: Protein fiber — extremely sensitive to enzymes, alkali, and heat
Dyes bleed easily — always test first

escalation.when: If lipstick stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is lipstick on silk, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Can you identify the specific components?
Is there a greasy/oily feel to the stain?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.

materialWarnings: ALWAYS test on a hidden seam or inside hem first — silk dyes may not tolerate spotting agents.
